answer: Three times

On the night of Ramchandra Ray's escape, Udayaditya is the one in command, ordering Sitaram to open the door and eventually binding him with cloth after Sitaram refuses to fight him. On the night of Udayaditya's own escape, the roles are reversed: Sitaram is the one who rescues Udayaditya by grabbing and carrying him out of the prison during a fire, and Udayaditya is helpless, asking Sitaram for guidance on what to do and where to go.

Pratapaditya's initial failed plan was to have two Pathan men kill Basanta Ray when he took shelter at the inn in Simultali on his way to Jessore. The method that finally succeeded was a written death sentence signed by Pratapaditya, which was delivered by Muktiyar Khan and carried out by a soldier named Abdul using a sword.

The jester Ramai Bhand faces physical retaliation in the inner quarters (specifically the Mahishi's room) while disguised as a middle-aged woman, and in the king's room while in the guise of the jester.
The Rajmahishi sent Matangini to secretly fetch poison from Mangala (Rukmini), which caused Surma's death.

The tragic consequence of Vibha's decision is that the king, Ramchandra Ray, feeling insulted by her refusal, decided to take another wife. Consequently, by the time Vibha eventually arrived at the royal palace, she discovered that the Maharaj was getting married, which caused her hopes of happiness to vanish.

Udayaditya gave his ring to Rukmini, who brought it to Sitaram and Bhagavat. Sitaram and Bhagavat used the ring's seal to stamp a forged petition that made it appear as if Udayaditya was lodging a complaint of treason against Pratapaditya with the Emperor. Bhagavat presented this forged petition to Pratapaditya, which led Pratapaditya to order Udayaditya's imprisonment.

Ramchandra Ray's escape involved Udayaditya overpowering and tying up guards, which led to the dismissal of Sitaram and Bhagavat. Udayaditya then provided monthly stipends to the dismissed guards, an act that enraged Pratapaditya. After Pratapaditya forbade further financial help, Surma secretly continued to send the stipends through a maid. When word of these secret stipends reached Pratapaditya, he ordered that Surma be sent to her paternal home.

The song signifies emptiness, loneliness, and the grief of being the only one remaining after those the singer loved are gone and no longer answer his call. Basanta Ray sings it in two contrasting contexts: first, humming it to himself while gazing at the setting sun on the veranda of his palace in Raigarh, and second, singing it while holding Vibha's hand after discovering that Surma's room in Jessore is silent and empty.

Rukmini's failure to stop the escape led her to later inform Pratapaditya that Udayaditya had escaped with the old king. She then accompanied Pratapaditya's men to Raigarh, where she successfully located Udayaditya. This discovery enabled Pratapaditya to send Muktiyar Khan to Raigarh with a signed order for the death sentence of the King of Raigarh, which resulted in Basanta Ray's execution.

Pratapaditya's initial reception of Ramchandra Ray was cold, indifferent, and dismissive; he refused to provide royal welcomes such as a lit path or the playing of the Nabat, and he ignored Ramchandra during their meeting to focus on ministerial business. In contrast, Ramchandra later allows and enjoys daily verbal attacks on Pratapaditya in his own court. His courtiers ridicule Pratapaditya's lineage, calling his grandfather an earthworm and his father a leech, while the minister and Ramai Bhand suggest that the marriage to Pratapaditya's daughter was a great fortune for the "mud" of Pratapaditya's family and that bringing her back into the house would lower its status.

Rukmini, who changed her name to Mangala and lived on the outskirts of Jessore, re-entered Udayaditya's life by befriending palace servants to monitor palace news and by using her knowledge of tantra, mantra, and medicine. She caused tragedy by preparing a poison, which was fetched by Matangini on behalf of the Rajmahishi, leading to Surma's death; the poison was specifically designed so that Udayaditya would feel no pity when Surma died. Later, she physically entered Udayaditya's bedchamber during a storm, manipulated him by claiming he had ruined her, and tricked him into giving her a ring.

Udayaditya's early lament stems from his feeling that he is unfit to bear the burden of the kingdom, a belief reinforced by his father's constant examination and his own failure to manage the Hosenkhali pargana at age sixteen, which led to disorder and decreased revenue. He expressed a wish that he had not been born as the crown prince but as the lowliest subject. This culmination occurs in Chapter 34 when, returning as a prisoner and viewing himself as a "weak coward" whose presence caused the destruction of others, he agrees with his father's statement that he is unworthy of the kingdom. He formally renounces the throne by swearing an oath before Ma-Kali and Pratapaditya that he will never accept any part of the kingdom, sit on the throne, or touch the royal scepter, designating Samaraditya as the heir and requesting to leave for Kashi to live as an ascetic.

Nayanchand intercepted the letter because he was afraid of Pratapaditya's reaction and gave it to the Mahishi instead. After Basanta Ray read the letter, he interpreted it as having been written only out of anger and advised the Mahishi to announce to the household that Ramchandra had written a letter requesting that Vibha be sent to Chandradwip. Consequently, the Mahishi told Vibha and announced to the household that Ramchandra had written a letter requesting that Vibha be sent to her in-laws' house.

Pratapaditya's attempt to trap Ramchandra Ray by blocking the canal adjoining the royal palace with large logs of Sal wood failed because Rammohan and his followers pulled the boat up and over the logs.

Basanta Ray's relationship with his sitar reflects his despair through his abandonment of the instrument; upon leaving Udayaditya, he leaves the sitar behind, stating he will not play it anymore. Later, he describes the sitar—once his "eternal companion"—as having broken strings that can no longer be tuned.

Earlier, Udayaditya fought against oppression with all his might and would have stopped officials from beating subjects to protect them without considering the consequences. After Surma's death, he lost this courage and was gripped by a blind fear of Pratapaditya. He now hesitates in every task; for example, when seeing a royal official beating a subject, he ran into his house instead of stopping the official, and he no longer has the courage to provide financial assistance to those in hardship, either openly or secretly.
Sitaram undoes the damage by going to Udayaditya's prison during a fire, telling him that the prison is on fire, and leading him out of the prison to freedom.

On the first occasion, Rammohan ties together thick, large sheets to create a huge rope, which he secures to a pillar on the palace roof; he then lifts Ramchandra onto his back, climbs down the rope, grips the rope with his teeth to carefully lower Ramchandra into a boat, and jumps down himself. On the second occasion, he lifts the unconscious and exhausted Vibha, brings her out of the palace, and places her into a palanquin near the gate.

In Jessore, Vibha is lavishly adorned by the Mahishi with eight thick gold bangles, one huge diamond bracelet on each arm, a large nose ring, and a specific coiffure, leaving her feeling burdened by these ornaments. In contrast, when she seeks Ramchandra Ray in Chandradwip, she rejects a palanquin to go as a "common subject" or "beggar-woman," dresses as a "common woman," and enters the palace "like any other maidservant," resulting in Ramchandra Ray mistaking her for a beggar woman seeking alms.

In his bedchamber after Surma's death, the window symbolizes loss and longing, as Udayaditya would sit there seeing the empty spot where Surma used to sit and hoping she would return. In the prison cell, the window symbolizes hope and liberation; by looking at the sky and flying birds through it, Udayaditya feels naturally free and believes that his "cage" will one day break and he will be released.

The dynamic began with Sitaram, a man with no money, visiting Rukmini (Mangala) to borrow money at interest. Their relationship was flirtatious, characterized by Sitaram's witty banter and the exchange of pet names such as "Pran," "Priye," and "Pranpriye," while Rukmini feigned affection. However, after Udayaditya's imprisonment, the relationship turned hostile; Sitaram cursed Rukmini and threatened to set her house on fire and grind her face on a stone floor. Later, believing Rukmini was dead, Sitaram entered her cottage to steal her money. When he discovered she was alive, he attempted to feign affection again, but Rukmini—consumed by rage—declared that she had returned from the door of Yama to lay Sitaram and the Yubaraj in an oven and smear her body with their ash. The encounter became life-threatening when Rukmini pursued Sitaram with a curved blade, striking the floor where he had stood and forcing him to flee Jessore with his family.

Sitaram's strategy begins as a distraction when he and his allies set fire to the guards' huts, causing the guards—including those at Udayaditya's door—to abandon their posts to save their belongings. To sustain the chaos, Sitaram's men hinder firefighting efforts by breaking water pots and pouring water in the wrong places. The strategy then evolves into a cover-up when his men set fire to Udayaditya's empty prison cell. Sitaram further cements the deception by throwing bones, a skull, and Udayaditya's sword into the burning room and fabricating reports that Udayaditya had been heard screaming before the roof collapsed, leading others to believe the Yubaraj had died in the fire.

Ramchandra Ray was not grateful for Udayaditya's actions, believing that saving him was "bound to happen" and that everyone would naturally unite to save him if he were in peril. Furthermore, he interpreted Udayaditya's main objective not as saving his own life, but as saving his own sister.

Pratapaditya initially dismisses the Emperor of Delhi's power as "empty fears," stating the Emperor is not his God and claiming that he himself is the "judge of the Emperor of Delhi." However, this political specter is later used against his son when Bhagavat and Sitaram forge a petition, stamped with the Yubaraj's seal, which makes it appear that the Yubaraj is lodging a complaint of treason against Pratapaditya with the Emperor in order to seize the kingdom for himself. Bhagavat delivers this forged petition to Pratapaditya, framing the son for betrayal.

Surma's presence is marked by Vibha secretly going to her to have her hair styled to her own liking, as she found the Mahishi's styling absolutely unbearable. Surma's subsequent tragic absence is marked by Vibha sitting quietly and waiting for Surma to braid her hair, which remains unbraided, and eventually becomes unkempt, leading Rammohan to observe that it seems there is no one left to care for her.
